title: Emergency Guide: My Dog Ingested Rat Poison
meta_desc: If your dog ate rat poison, this is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn what to do immediately to save your pet's life.
content_html:
TL;DR: Rodenticide ingestion is a life-threatening medical emergency requiring immediate veterinary care, even if your dog appears normal. Providing a photo of the poison packaging allows your vet to identify the specific toxin and start the correct treatment immediately.

What happens if my dog eats rat or mouse poison?

  • Rodenticide ingestion occurs when a dog consumes poisonous bait intended for rats or mice.
  • These products contain various toxic chemicals such as anticoagulants, which prevent blood from clotting, or neurotoxins like bromethalin.
  • Because these baits are often flavored to be attractive to pests, they are unfortunately also appealing to dogs.

Is rodenticide ingestion a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a critical medical emergency that can be fatal if not treated immediately.
  • Rat poison is designed to be lethal, and the toxic effects often have a delayed onset.
  • Even if your dog is acting normally right now, early decontamination and treatment by a veterinarian are essential for a positive prognosis.

How does providing a photo of the poison packaging help my veterinarian?

  • Providing a photo of the product packaging or the ingredient label is one of the most helpful things you can do.
  • Different types of rat poison affect the body in completely different ways, such as causing internal bleeding or brain swelling.
  • Identifying the specific active ingredient allows your veterinarian to administer the correct antidote or treatment plan immediately upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of potential rat poison ingestion in dogs, prompt decontamination is critical. For bromethalin ingestion at dosages of 0.1-0.49 mg/kg in dogs, emesis induced via 3% hydrogen peroxide or apomorphine within 4 hours of exposure may be adequate; if unsuccessful or if >4 hours have elapsed, administer activated charcoal at 1-2 g/kg body weight, being mindful of aspiration risk and potential hypernatremia. For zinc phosphide ingestion, induce emesis with apomorphine if the animal has not already vomited. Decreasing gastric acid with oral magnesium hydroxide or famotidine (1 mg/kg SC) may be beneficial. While the animal is under observation, provide IV fluid support. Activated charcoal may be considered, but avoid if vomiting is ongoing due to aspiration risk. Baseline biochemical profiles with repeat liver and renal value evaluations at 24, 48, and possibly 72 hours are recommended. For suspected anticoagulant rodenticide toxicosis, a prolonged prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) with normal fibrinogen, fibrin degradation products, and platelet counts are suggestive, as is a positive response to vitamin K1. Stomach contents, serum, or plasma can be analyzed for the presence of anticoagulant to confirm diagnosis.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3168)

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically directed to do so by a veterinarian or poison control specialist, as it can sometimes lead to aspiration pneumonia or other complications.

faqs.question: How long does it take for symptoms to show?
faqs.answer: Symptoms can appear within hours or take several days to manifest depending on the type of poison. Waiting for symptoms to appear is dangerous; immediate treatment is required.

faqs.question: What if I only suspect they ate it?
faqs.answer: If there is any possibility of ingestion, treat it as an emergency. It is far safer to have a veterinarian examine the dog than to risk the poison being absorbed into their system.
